original_hypothesis.title: USDCAdapter Burn Requires Direct User Allowance Causing Bridging Reverts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When bridging USDC back to the canonical chain, ERC20Vault.sendToken calls BridgedERC20(token).burn(msg.sender, amount). For the USDCAdapter, _burnToken uses usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount). This requires the user to have approved the USDCAdapter contract directly, rather than the ERC20Vault.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This breaks the standard bridging UX where users approve the Vault contract. If frontends or other smart contracts assume the standard flow (approve Vault -> call Vault.sendToken), the transaction will revert because the USDCAdapter lacks allowance. This creates a deterministic DoS for automated systems integrating with the bridge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount) requires direct allowance to adapter

preconditions: User attempts to bridge USDC back to canonical chain via ERC20Vault
User approved ERC20Vault instead of USDCAdapter

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: transferFrom called by Adapter using user's address as _from
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes integration failures and stuck transactions

economic_questions: How will frontends know to approve the Adapter instead of the Vault?
Does this break EIP-2612 permit flows if integrated?
validation_requirements: Test bridging USDC with Vault allowance vs Adapter allowance

validation_result.mechanism: non-standard approval target in USDCAdapter burn
validation_result.impact_summary: Users who approve ERC20Vault (standard bridging UX) cannot bridge USDC back because USDCAdapter._burnToken requires user approval to the adapter contract, not the vault. Transactions revert deterministically.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies a code defect in the USDCAdapter._burnToken implementation. When a user bridges USDC back to the canonical chain via ERC20Vault.sendToken, the call chain is: ERC20Vault.sendToken -> _handleMessage -> IBridgedERC20(USDCAdapter).burn(user, amount) -> BridgedERC20Base.burn -> USDCAdapter._burnToken(user, amount) -> usdc.transferFrom(user, adapter, amount). The USDC transferFrom is called by the USDCAdapter contract (msg.sender = USDCAdapter from USDC's perspective), meaning the user must have approved the USDCAdapter contract. However, the standard bridging UX requires users to approve only the ERC20Vault. For regular BridgedERC20 tokens, _burnToken calls the internal ERC20._burn function which doesn't require any allowance. The USDCAdapter breaks this pattern by requiring an external transferFrom that needs a separate approval to a different contract address.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at USDCAdapter.sol:45-48 is correct. The _burnToken function at those lines calls usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount), which requires _from (the user) to have approved address(this) (the USDCAdapter). Meanwhile, ERC20Vault._handleMessage at ERC20Vault.sol lines ~320-323 calls IBridgedERC20(_token).burn(msg.sender, _amount) where _token is the USDCAdapter. The BridgedERC20Base.burn function at BridgedERC20Base.sol lines ~70-82 verifies the caller is the vault, then delegates to _burnToken. The mismatch is clear: users interact with and approve the ERC20Vault, but the actual transferFrom requires approval to the USDCAdapter.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount) is called where msg.sender is the USDCAdapter. For this to succeed, _from (the user) must have approved the USDCAdapter contract. But in the standard bridging flow, users approve ERC20Vault, not the adapter.

The vault calls burn on the USDCAdapter passing the user address. Unlike regular BridgedERC20 which uses internal _burn (no allowance needed), USDCAdapter requires external transferFrom with user approval to the adapter.

calling_sequence: 1. User approves ERC20Vault for USDC spending (standard UX)
2. User calls ERC20Vault.sendToken(_op) where _op.token = USDCAdapter address
3. ERC20Vault._handleMessage checks bridgedToCanonical[_token].addr != address(0) → true for USDCAdapter
8. USDC checks allowance[user][USDCAdapter] which is 0 → REVERTS
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core issue is in step 7-8: usdc.transferFrom is called with msg.sender = USDCAdapter, requiring the user to have approved the USDCAdapter. But users in the standard flow only approve the ERC20Vault (step 1). The BridgedERC20._burnToken uses internal _burn() which needs no allowance, but USDCAdapter wraps an external USDC contract requiring explicit approval to the adapter.

deployment_assumptions_checked: USDCAdapter deployed and registered via changeBridgedToken
config_validation.deployment_evidence: DeployUSDCAdapter.s.sol confirms the USDCAdapter is deployed and registered via ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken. The deployment script sets up the adapter as a bridged token for USDC, confirming it will be used in the sendToken flow.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario but a deterministic integration failure. Any user or automated system that follows the standard bridging pattern (approve ERC20Vault → call sendToken) will have their transaction revert when bridging USDC back. The user must separately discover they need to approve the USDCAdapter contract specifically. Frontends that don't account for this special case will produce failing transactions.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. All USDC bridging-back operations fail if users follow the standard approval flow. This affects every user attempting to bridge USDC from L2 to L1 via the standard ERC20Vault.sendToken path. The workaround exists (approve USDCAdapter directly), but it breaks the standard UX pattern and requires special-case handling in frontends and integrating contracts.
recommended_mitigations: Modify USDCAdapter._burnToken to not require user approval - e.g., have the ERC20Vault transfer USDC to the adapter first, then have the adapter burn it
Alternative: Override burn() in USDCAdapter to first pull USDC from the user to the vault, then to the adapter, leveraging the vault's existing approval
At minimum, document the non-standard approval requirement prominently and ensure all frontends handle this special case
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy USDCAdapter with mock USDC. 2. Register USDCAdapter as bridged token via changeBridgedToken. 3. User approves ERC20Vault for USDC. 4. User calls ERC20Vault.sendToken with USDCAdapter token. 5. Observe revert due to insufficient allowance on USDCAdapter. 6. Then approve USDCAdapter and retry - should succeed.
uncertainties: Frontend implementations may already handle this special case, though the on-chain code still has the inconsistency
If USDC has any special transferFrom behavior beyond standard ERC20, there could be additional complications
additional_testing_needed: End-to-end test of USDC bridging with only ERC20Vault approval vs USDCAdapter approval
Test that ERC20 permit (EIP-2612) flows also handle this correctly if integrated
Verify behavior with real USDC contract on testnet
